title: Queue
general_notes: No double deduction (except in point 6). Do not deduct again for an error already penalised in an earlier point. For example: if enqueue() is incorrect, base dequeue() marks on whether it consistently reads from the position the student's enqueue() wrote to. HOWEVER, for the overall correctness (point 6), there is double deduction for an incorrect result even if earlier steps were internally consistent (4th bullet point in number 6).
This rubric applies to both Java and Python submissions. Any correct internal representation that implements FIFO semantics (e.g., array with index tracking, dynamic list) is acceptable.
Java uses the method name emptyqueue(); Python uses isEmpty(). Both are correct. Accept either name.
The use of 'import' is not allowed. Penalise in point 6 if 'import' appears in the submission.

rubric_points.name: Class structure and internal storage
rubric_points.target: The Queue class is defined and contains internal storage and any necessary tracking state sufficient to maintain elements in FIFO order.

buckets.label: Wrong
buckets.description: No Queue class defined, or class is defined but contains no internal storage or state fields whatsoever.

buckets.label: Semi
buckets.description: A Queue class is defined and has some internal storage, but it is incomplete or insufficient to correctly support all four queue operations (e.g., storage present but no tracking state, or tracking state present but no storage).

buckets.label: Correct
buckets.description: A Queue class is defined with complete internal storage and all tracking state needed to implement FIFO behaviour. Correctness of individual methods is evaluated in points 2 to 5.

rubric_points.name: emptyqueue() / isEmpty(): correct emptiness check
rubric_points.target: The method returns true if and only if the queue contains no elements, based on the internal state of the queue.

buckets.label: Wrong
buckets.description: Method is missing, always returns a constant value regardless of queue state, or checks a condition unrelated to whether the queue is empty.

buckets.label: Semi
buckets.description: Method attempts to check emptiness using the internal state but the condition is incorrect, giving wrong results for some queue states.

buckets.label: Correct
buckets.description: Returns true if and only if the queue contains no elements, using a condition that correctly reflects the current internal state.

rubric_points.name: head(): returns front element without modifying queue
rubric_points.target: head() returns the element at the front of the queue without removing it or modifying any internal state.

buckets.label: Wrong
buckets.description: Method is missing, returns an element from the wrong end of the queue, or always returns a default value regardless of queue contents.

buckets.label: Semi
buckets.description: Returns the correct element but also modifies internal state (i.e., behaves like dequeue rather than a pure read), or reads from the correct end but uses an incorrect index or offset.

buckets.label: Correct
buckets.description: Returns the front element of the queue without advancing any pointer, removing any element, or modifying the queue in any way.

rubric_points.name: enqueue(x): adds element to the back of the queue
rubric_points.target: enqueue(x) inserts the element at the back of the queue and correctly updates all internal tracking state so subsequent operations remain consistent.

buckets.label: Wrong
buckets.description: Method is missing, does not store the element in the internal structure, or inserts at the wrong position (e.g., prepends instead of appending to the back).

buckets.label: Semi
buckets.description: Stores the element at the correct end but does not fully update the internal tracking state afterwards, causing subsequent operations to fail or produce incorrect results.

buckets.label: Correct
buckets.description: Correctly inserts the element at the back of the queue and updates all tracking state so that subsequent head(), dequeue(), and emptyqueue() calls continue to work correctly.

rubric_points.name: dequeue(): removes and returns the front element
rubric_points.target: dequeue() retrieves the element at the front of the queue, removes it, updates all internal tracking state, and returns the retrieved element.

buckets.label: Wrong
buckets.description: Method is missing, does not retrieve the correct element, or returns the value without removing it from the internal structure.

buckets.label: Semi
buckets.description: Retrieves and returns the correct element and attempts removal, but the internal tracking state is not fully or correctly updated, leaving the queue in an inconsistent state for subsequent operations.

buckets.label: Correct
buckets.description: Correctly retrieves the front element, removes it from the internal structure, updates all tracking state, and returns the retrieved value so that subsequent operations on the queue remain consistent.

rubric_points.name: Overall correctness of Queue
rubric_points.target: All four methods work correctly together: the queue preserves FIFO order across repeated enqueue and dequeue operations, and does not use 'import'.

buckets.label: Wrong
buckets.description: Class fails to compile or run, or most methods produce incorrect output.

buckets.label: Semi
buckets.description: Basic FIFO behaviour is correct for simple cases but fails under repeated or boundary operations (e.g., many enqueue and dequeue calls, reuse of freed storage), or 'import' is present in the submission.

buckets.label: Correct
buckets.description: All methods work correctly together across all test cases. FIFO order is preserved under repeated operations and no 'import' is present.
